turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: This case involves no issue of fact -- fraction but only a naked question of constitutional law. The issue is simply whether Congress has power to cut off aliens' Social Security benefits when they are deported on the basis of membership in the Communist Party. The case comes here on direct appeal from the District Court for the District of Columbia which held that Mr. Nestor's Social Security rights had become vested and could not be terminated. Although this Court postponed the question of jurisdiction until argument, the appeal appears to fall squarely within the word and the spirit of the jurisdictional statute which authorizes direct appeals from a District Court decision holding an Act of Congress unconstitutional in a case in which the United States is a party. The only apparent question on jurisdiction is whether or not the suit below should have been before a three-judge court rather than below -- before a single judge. But this suit was instituted by Mr. Nestor pursuant to the specific provisions of the Social Security Act which provides a remedy for review of administrative decisions by a District Court and makes no provision for a three-judge court in that case. The three-judge court provision of course comes into effect when someone attempts to enjoin the operation or execution of an Act of Congress. It does not control where the action is under a specific review provision such as this and where there is no injunction against the operation of the Security Act -- Social Security Act involved. Therefore, it seems clear to us that the District Court is a single judge, did have jurisdiction. And since his action was specifically based upon unconstitutionality of an Act of Congress, this Court in turn appears to have jurisdiction. Ephram Nestor came to this country from Bulgaria as a young man in 1913.For some reason, he never was naturalized. But in 1933, he joined the Communist Party and he remained a member of that party at least until 1939. On the basis of this Communist Party membership -- preceding this were commenced against him in 1953 to deport him to Bulgaria which was the country from which he had come. And he was, in fact, deported in July of 1956. Meanwhile, he had qualified for Social Security benefits and in fact, for several months had received Social Security benefits amounting to $55.60 a month.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That was between 1953 and 1956 that he qualified for the benefits?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, he actually qualified as of November of 1955 and the first -- it was the -- the order came in 1956 just before he was deported. So he received the payments for November of 1955, December and end of 1956 until he was deported. As a specific --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How long had he been a member of the system, Mr. Smith?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: He -- he has started an employment which was subject to social security in about 1936, I believe, it was early. He was -- he was a painter by profession and some of his earlier employment came soon after the Act was enacted. As was specifically provided by a 1954 amendment to the immigration law, the Attorney General notified the Social Security Administration when Nestor was deported. And this is also specifically required by the law. The payments for Social Security payments were then terminated. Neither of these actions is subject to discretion. It's automatically under the statutes that when a man is deported for this reason that his payment should -- shall be terminated. Mr. Nestor appealed the termination of his -- of his -- of these benefits. He was granted a hearing before a referee in the Social Security Administration. The -- the decision was upheld by the referee and after having exhausted his administrative remedies, he then went to the District Court and asked for review of this decision. And as I mentioned, the District Court held that the statutory provision cutting off the benefits was unconstitutional and it is from that out -- the Government has appealed directly to this Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where was the statute dealing with cutting off?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is Section 10 to -- Section (n), it's -- appears at page 9 -- 91 of our brief.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: 202 in -- of the Act.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was it illegal for him to be a member of the party during the years he was a member?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I don't -- we don't know the number -- the years that he actually was a member but presumably not. At least no -- there's no basis of illegality, no allegation of illegality back in 1936. We don't have any termination date on -- on his membership. He was a member at least until 1939 and as of that date, there would be no change in the laws. The basic questions are, first, whether Congress has the power to reduce or eliminate benefits once stranded. And the second question, if so, were the cutting off benefits to deported aliens under these circumstances is a proper exercise of that power.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May -- may I trouble with one more thing. This Section 202 (n), is that it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was that in the original enactment?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, this was enacted in 1954. It was an amendment to the Act in 1954.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Amendment to the Act. And up to that -- up to that time, there was no -- no provision, implication or anything else?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, this is subsequent to the beginning of his earned social security.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. He was -- he was -- presumably paid taxes from about 1936 until his -- until his employment ceased. And that was before his taxes were -- before this was found in the Act.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And -- and this, I think (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, that is right.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And came into the law after (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: After the proceedings were instituted but before there was any finality to any order of deportation. That's right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Does the statute provide for any refunds to him of what he's paid in?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, there is no refund given and -- in -- in this connection. I address myself first to the question of whether -- generally, without regard to the specific grounds for termination whether generally, Congress has the power to reduce or eliminate payments once they've been included in an act and once taxes have been paid under the Act. Now, Congress in so many words has provided that it retains the power to amend or appeal any provision of the Act. And in fact, the provisions of the Act have been repeatedly amended since it was first passed in 1935.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is that in the brief?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's at page 92 and 93 of my brief that begins with the paragraph numbered 4 at the very bottom of page 92. It's a short section, the right to alter, amend or appeal any provision of this Act of Congress. Most of the amendments to be sure have been amendments to increase payments, increase benefits under the Social Security Act. But in -- as an incident of this, there have been many cases where -- as to individuals, their benefits have been reduced or eliminated because of readjustments in the Act. And in the lower courts wherever this has been litigated, the power to change the Act has been -- has been upheld. And indeed it seems essential that Congress should have this power since otherwise the -- the Act would be frozen in its original form from the way it was passed. And Congress certainly never had had intended any such result. The only reason that there's any -- any question about it is that this is a contributory system and the individual pays -- has made contributions before the changes. And it's in -- it's important in this respect to know that these contributions are specifically in the nature of taxes. The -- they have made as a part of the tax law and the payments which a man receives are not in any manner conditioned upon his having paid taxes and his having paid taxes in no way gives him a right to -- by itself, to qualify for payments under the system. In other words, the system is financed through a -- through taxing the -- the labor force for the idea that they will support the Social Security System as a whole. But there is no direct relationship in the law between the taxes which any individual pay -- pays and the benefits which he receives. Congress purposely tied the -- the financing of the program onto taxes of the labor force because they wanted it felt that Social Security benefits were not charity, that they were not in the nature of -- of relief that labor was paying for its own -- its -- its own way in this thing.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: When you say taxing the labor force, individuals are taxed.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I suppose for each individual, there's a separate fiscal account. I don't know where in Social -- is there?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To their general -- to their general fund that these -- the amounts collected both from an employer and employee go into a general fund, do they?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, not -- not -- there -- there is a trust fund that covers the whole Social Security System but there is no fund for an individual. They --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, no, no. But there is a -- that's what I meant by general fund.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But there's no record even of the taxes as far as the individual is concerned. What they do is to keep an employment record in order to determine what his rights will be, but his rights are not dependent upon taxes having been paid on his employment.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No. But if he -- if he -- suppose he doesn't pay his taxes, what -- what are the consequences?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They'll attempt to collect them from him.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They -- they will attempt to collect them.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Actually, his employer withholds them as -- as you know in the employer.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It isn't that his insurance that is the Social Security benefit fees, do they? The Government doesn't go at it that way.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, they -- no, but on the other hand, if they do not collect them for some reason, if he resist the payment of them --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and -- and for some reason, they are never --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or overlooked.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- collected. He still is entitled to his Social Security benefits as long as he has under covered employment during this time.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Does he lose his job?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: If he doesn't pay in.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It has no relationship to his -- to his job.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: This particular payment has nothing to do with his job security.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. Well -- well, of course, we're dealing now with non-government employment. Of course, this is private -- this is Social Security in private employment.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And so the -- there's no reason -- there's no federal impact on his job whether he pays or doesn't pay. It's not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As a matter of fact, a good many of the employees' taxes are paid by the employer, as we know very widely. Certainly, it's true --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Certainly in the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in certain classes of -- of --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- in the domestic field, it must be -- be --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the domestic belongs entirely.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It belongs entirely. But what --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Does the Government make any contributions there?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, the entire system is financed through the taxes on the employer and the employees. It's supposed to be divided half and half, except in the case of self-employment where of course -- which is now under the system too where the entire contribution comes from the person who is self-employed.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you think it makes a constitutional distinction that they call these payments taxes instead of something else?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, I don't think that they -- the fact they call them taxes or put them in the tax law is the determinative factor. But the fact that these are not like insurance premiums or annuity premiums which give a contractual right and the right isn't -- isn't tied in to the payment, does seem to me to make a material difference.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, doesn't the Act call it insurance?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The Act does call it insurance.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, why -- why then do we have to take the -- the tax definition of it rather than insurance?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, because this does not have the features which you generally have within insurance -- insurance policy. I mean there's no right of return, no building up of a -- of a surrender fund, the -- the ordinary features which you find in private insurance are not present here. This is Social Security insurance in -- in a -- in a sense rather than a commercial type of insurance.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: They pay every month, don't they?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They -- they --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Like we do in insurance?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They pay every month -- they every week if --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, to whom?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- they were told -- they were told whatever pay -- whatever pay there is.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And they get a benefit --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- for having paid it, whatever it might be.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. But in the same way that they pay income taxes and get benefits from income taxes. These things have to be financed in some way and these are -- are financed through this particular kind of a tax.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean they get benefits in the same sense here that they do from paying their income tax?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: In -- in -- basically yes, they -- I don't mean that the income tax finances the Social Security System, but citizens of the United States get certain benefits and -- and rights from the Federal Government. And these are financed through their paying taxes. Their rights are not dependent upon their paying of taxes. They -- they -- that they -- the taxes were enforced against them regardless of whether they get the rights and they get the rights regardless of whether they pay the taxes. And the same is true in this situation.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Davis, what is the statutory characterization of these benefits? I had thought that it was, number one, unemployment compensation, and second, old-age benefits. Was that not the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- statutory characterization?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Then it's not called insurance, is it or isn't it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it is -- it is. It was the original Act --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) want compensation insurance?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. It was in the -- in the original Act but when they amended it in 1939, the word insurance -- for example, you get the -- the -- well, the term insurance financing old-age and survivors disability insurance, the term insurance works into the Act in the 1939 amendment. It wasn't then in 1935 and there wasn't any real change in the nature of it, but there was a desire -- there was a desire at that time to tell these people that they were paying their own way that this wasn't charity. And I think that's where they put the term -- use this term.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is this a -- a truly contributory system in the sense that what you get depends on dollars paid?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. Indeed, it doesn't. The -- the -- actually, the -- in the early days particularly, what you get -- what people got was not dependent upon what they paid but from what was being paid currently and that by and large is the way the system works that -- that financial itself as you go along and the present labor force is really paying for the Social Security benefits which the older and retired people are getting at the present time.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, what's the $22 billions in the trust fund for?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: What's it for? It's -- it's used -- the funds are used from that to -- to finance the payments and -- and that --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I thought you just said that the current take takes care of the current outlets.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I -- this is -- this has been built up through the years and it is used. For a long time, it was increasing. This fund was increasing year by year. In the last several years, it's been somewhat --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, then --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- dropped down. But you get --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- then the income falls (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- you get the income from that and you get an appropriation by Congress and the two were used for the payment of the -- of the current --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But the current payments that is collected with the taxes or what they are from employees and current collection rather and employers do not aggregate enough to pay the benefits currently --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. I -- I should correct myself a little. These payments do not actually go into the -- into the trust fund. What happens is the payments which are collected from the employee and the employer go into the general treasury. But there is a general appropriation whereby the same amount is appropriated from funds not otherwise used and goes into the trust fund so that the amount that goes into the trust fund is actually measured by the amounts which are collected.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And the demands for benefit, as I -- I gather, it's calculated in time, will exceed the tax payments by such --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- amount that there have to be drawings on the so-called trust fund, is that it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. Except the -- they are also based -- they -- they use the interest from that to supplement it. The -- the tax payments are going up. The tax payments are now 5% or maybe 6%. They're going up -- they figured it to 9% before they get the -- before they have the system really have enough tax system to cover it with the insurance.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, now, further word about the financing of it. It is -- that the funds supposedly are expected to derive from the tax on the employer in the labor force. Suppose to some unimaginable reasons, each unimaginable by means, that those funds are not sufficient for what under the law required to be the Social Security benefit. Is there an obligation under the statute to pay out the fixed schedules --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, they --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to direct appropriations by commerce?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, they dig into this fund, the -- the interest from the fund --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but that fund --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and then in to the fund.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But that --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They used up the fund --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the fund came from the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- practical resources. Suppose for some reason, there's a miscalculation by the Secretary of Treasury, that's imaginable and there is a deficit, is that -- is that automatic as Congress appropriates so as to keep the benefits going?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I imagine they would. We haven't faced that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There would be no money that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I know but the scheme, the scheme required appropriations by Congress?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The annual appropriations by Congress is -- that is right. And if there weren't enough, well, Congress would either have to abandon Social Security or appropriate more money funds. The --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, don't you have some pretty strong language to get over it? Take your Appendix A on page 89 where you quote the Act. The title of it is “Old-age and Survivors Insurance Benefit Payments.” Old-age Insurance Benefits. Every individual who is a fully insured individual in Section 214 (a) and has attained retirement age and has filed an application for old-age insurance benefits shall be entitled to an old-age insurance benefit for each month beginning. That's a pretty strong language, isn't it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is this insurance --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is as the term “insurance” repeatedly and insured repeatedly.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, you -- you wipe that all out.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, the -- the actual legal incidence of this are not the same as we think of as insurance. Maybe -- maybe we'd do better not to argue -- maybe I would do better not to argue about whether this is insurance but rather to raise the question of whether there was any contractual right, whether a man has -- by making his payments, any contractual right with the Government to a return of -- of benefits. And I think that if we put it on that basis, it's perfectly clear under the Act that there is no contract right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean by that (Inaudible) you mean by that -- that we can forget about whether it's insurance or a tax.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well -- well, what I -- I -- that -- I had originally stated it in terms of tax against insurance but I think that the only reason that that is important is because insurance is generally thought of as a contractual right. And if we attack the basis whether or not it is a contractual right, we no longer have to worry about whether it's insurance.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I just want to ask you this just -- I don't want to interrupt you but are you -- is your defense based wholly on the idea that the Government has a right to cancel for the reasons that exist in this case or that the Government's right to amend gives it the right to repeal the whole law and abolish the point if it so desire?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, up -- up to the present time, I was attacking -- I was approaching the general proposition they have a right to abandon the whole -- the whole scheme. They have abandoned a right to change the entire scheme.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Or to do a way with it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Or to do a way with it. And --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That's what I thought.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and now, when -- eventually, I shall come to the problem that this particular cutting off is an appropriate exercise of that power to -- to change and that's a different question. That's a -- that's a question of whether this is appropriate legislation.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Have they ever cut off any particular group before this?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Not before this. Since this Act -- since this section has been passed, there have been two other sections which deal with -- generally, with cutting off groups. One of them is with respect to aliens who voluntarily go abroad and stay for more than six months. And if they haven't been in this country for 10 years, then their rights are cut off. And the second section provides for cutting off the Social Security benefits of individuals who are convicted of espionage, sabotage in -- in the Smith Act cases. Those are the specific --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Three. There are three --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Those are the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- sections.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- present provisions in the Act. The -- these last two haven't been passed upon by -- by the support applied. There have, however, been -- as I say, been incidence where other adjustments in the Act have resulted in individuals losing their rights. Let me give you an example. For a long time, self-insure -- self-employed people were not subject to the tax and their self-employment wasn't counted in, in determining whether they were retired. It's the so-called “work test”. And in order to qualify for payments, why, you have to be retired. But when -- when self-employment wasn't covered, you could be retired and -- and practice law, for example, on your own. Then the law was changed to bring self-employed people within the scope of the Act. And as a result, some people who had had benefits and at -- from employment and had retired and then supplementing that with their own income, were now -- were now cut off from their benefits because they were self-employed and these earnings were now considered as -- as part of the -- the work test. This was -- this was litigated and the Court -- and never got beyond the District Court but the District Court held that this was an appropriate amendment by -- by Congress and that rights could be cut off in this nature, in this manner. I may say there's been some experience too with the state retirement systems and there is a case, Pennie against Reis, an -- an old case, 132 United States in which it was held that -- I think that was the California retirement systems holding that they could cut off benefits even though they had once been received within general statutory power to amend.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was there employee contribution then?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, sir, I think there was.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: When was that?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I -- on -- I think there was a contribution.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: When was that, I say?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: When was it?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you say 132 U.S.?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that's great I assume.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, it's a long time ago.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I didn't know of any --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: We have -- we --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I didn't know of any retirement system that far back in California. There had been.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I don't think I have the data but -- but it is (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) that's was a turn of the century, isn't it?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Then this -- we have collected these cases dealing with the state system on page -- systems on page 56, 57 and 58 of our brief. Pennie against Reis was a -- was a -- I think he was a policeman.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, could been, yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I think he was a policeman --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Could've been before back.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- and I think he was in California.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- I don't want to interfere --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- with the orderly development of your argument and -- and if my question does postpone it until you find it fitting. Do you rely heavily on the explicit reservation of the power to amend, to repeal, etcetera?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I certainly rely on it, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But I think it would be the same even if it weren't there.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that doesn't --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I mean this is making specific what is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- essential to make the system work anyway.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, except we are told sometimes it is important to spell things out.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, in that -- to that -- in that respect, that's --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Davis, do I correctly read your adversary's brief that they're not defending the basis of the holding below, namely, that this was a vested interest before it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes. That -- that's quite true. In several places in that brief, I don't think they would quarrel with what -- with what I have said this day.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's what I wanted to --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They might in some details but basically, they don't claim that you cannot change that this is the contract right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's -- it's 1878.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And they don't claim that this was a vested interest, aren't they?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They do not claim it's a vested interest. They say that this particular cutting off is a punishment and it's unconstitutional. That's -- that's --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) on the holding that it was vested.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: He said it was a vested interest. That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Did you allow --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This fund is public fund subject to the legislative control.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Did you allow mentioning it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that they'll be -- had no vested interest in that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There's language in Lynch against the United States. That however is not -- that's a more --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I think that -- I understood you to say they're not raising that question.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And we have (Inaudible)

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: It was raised in Lynch against the United States.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- but which could not know.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They -- they're not claiming it's a contract in this case. That is right. I -- I believe they're not and Mr. Rein would -- would be in a position [Laughs] -- he have the last word on that but his brief -- in his brief, there are several cases where they admit that there can be changes in these rights as long as they're appropriate changes and comes with the Social Security System. And that brings us to what, I think, is -- is the most -- more difficult question in the case and that is whether to cut off an alien's rights for Social Security business -- benefits on the basis of his being deported as a member of the Communist Party, whether this is an appropriate -- appropriate exercise of the -- of the Government's right to change the Social Security law.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Would you go so far as to say the Government could -- say the State Government could confiscate his house and property and his real estate?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I would certainly say it couldn't. I mean, I think that Wong Wing proved -- establishes the fact that the alien has a right to due process, liberty and property and that they cannot be taken away on deportation.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Even though he has been lawfully deported.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. It is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) question is raised that a special class of deportee (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, there is. There -- the -- the -- it is -- it is argued basically. The -- the argument is that this is a punishment because of the classes of deportees that are involved. This is a punishment which is being imposed upon this particular class. And for that reason, it is unconstitutional as lacking in due process.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, there is -- actually, Mr. Davis, not all classes of deportees were -- are cut off.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And what -- at least four omitted in the original law?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. There were -- there are 18 classes of deportees and of those 14 are cut off and four are not cut off.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Still not cut off.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What are those or what --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: If you'll turn to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) equal protection.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. The -- the deportation statute appears on page 96 through 101 of our brief and I had marked the sections which are the reasons for deportation which are not -- not cut off. And the first one is number three on page 96, which is that the person has become a -- institutionalized as a public charge at public expense. That's number three on page 96.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Is it cut off or not cut off?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Is number three cut off or not cut off?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: It is not a -- he retained -- he still gets his Social Security.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: He still gets.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There are 14 where they cut off and four where they're not and I'm mentioning the four which are not cut off. The next one is number eight on page 100, eight on page 100 which also deals with becoming a public charge. And the next one is nine on page 101 which is failing to maintain a nonimmigrant status, it would be a situation where someone came in here and -- a student or --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- something like that and went to work. And the last one is 13 which is at the bottom of page 101 running all to 102 and that deals with anyone who -- within five years, aids or assists another alien to enter the United States in violation of law.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can you generalize those four? You said that the four (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I -- I really find it --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- difficult to make a great --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It all seems to me on the (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I -- I find it -- well, the -- the last one, 13 and probably nine deal with --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, nine and 13 probably deal with violations of law. I mean, I think it's probably a violation for anyone who's admitted as a student, for example, to go to work. At least, he can -- he's -- he's doing something he has promised not to do. 13 is definitely a -- a --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Nine and 13, if I may correct you, nine and 13 (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They're not -- they're not as -- they're not in the same class with some of the others. But on the other hand, some of these other --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) one as the first one. It is (Inaudible) both three and there's another one (Inaudible) with the public charge.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Those cases seem (Inaudible) in -- in others.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But can't you say some of the things they -- some of things (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Some of the others are also not very serious. I -- I am not able to find any -- the Congress hasn't helped us in this. They haven't told us why they dropped these four out and why they didn't. The -- the appellant says that this shows that these are -- that the ones where this cutoff are punishments. I don't think it shows that. I think some of these things where it's not cut off, if it were you could say it was a punishment.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, you would say some attentive distinctions in your brief. Are you abandoning those?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, I won't. Those are the best we can do and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: It's not a very good thing, probably. [Laughter]

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, he hasn't thought of any of those.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Certainly, there's a great deal of --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: [Laughs]

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) one, two, four, five and others -- and the other ones. I think there are different force that are narrow -- has narrow significance (Inaudible) I offer you those facts.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes. Well, that -- that maybe -- that maybe it, except that hurts my case more than it helps it, if I may say.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) give you less reasons. [Laughter]

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Penalize them (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, this --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- from this argument.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- from that argument, that's right. So, that is one of the reasons I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I don't see that reason in the -- in the statute. It's the Government's position that Congress can cut off this kind of benefits for good reasons or for bad reasons, but since these are privileges, you cannot cut them off for reasons which are forbidden by the Constitution. One could not make -- receive the benefits under the Social Security Act depend, for example, upon race or religion or upon politics because these are bases of classification which the -- which the Constitution denies to Congress. But on the other hand, it seems as though they -- no reason why they cannot make a differentiation on the basis of alienage, particularly alienage when it's tied up with an alien who is no longer living in this country. It's long been recognized that if an alien comes here and does not undertake the duties of citizenship that he remains a guest here and that he doesn't have the full rights, privileges of -- of citizens. He can be deported, he can be denied privileges which are given to the people who -- who are full citizens.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is that the bill of attainder put on him?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Could that be a bill of attainder put on him --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. There could not --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- prior to the grant?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: There could not be a bill of attainder put on him because the Constitution forbids a bill of attainder and the constitutional rights cannot be taken away from him, but privileges which are granted to citizens as citizens can be withheld from him.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: As far as this is the right to practice law?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Probably not. There are -- there are statutes, state statutes with respect to employment and it's held that -- I don't know about the statute of -- they're just practice of law and probably could be withheld from an alien as a reasonable thing that --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I mean as a bill of attainder?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, no, not a -- not the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: By legislative acts?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No, no. The -- this Court itself is -- is cast upon the question of -- of taking away a right to practice as a -- for a (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And do you think that the bill of attainder, an alien has a right to protect himself against bills of attainder?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right. If this is a bill of attainder, if this is --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- a bill of attainder, this is -- this, we think, is unconstitutional.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: An ex post facto law. (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Again, if this is properly an ex post facto law, again, it is unconstitutional and it cannot be done.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, that's really the argument. Your adversary -- there is two of the --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Those are the arguments that they are making and this in turn comes down to whether or not this is a reasonable exercise of congressional power in connection with federal purposes and -- and the system and the -- and the Social Security System or whether this is a method of punishing. All of these questions right to jury trial, ex post facto or bills of attainder, all depend upon this being a punishment.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that -- that is more the good things on. This -- it can't be ex post facto unless you overruled all the -- the whole picture of (Inaudible) ex post facto merely involved in criminal statutes pursuable as a crime substitute of the Sixth Amendment.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- that is -- that's right. It has -- this has to be a penal statute in order for --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's not in the loose sense a penal statute.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I don't think that makes you smart or heard. It has to be --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: A punishment.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- a punishment through this criminal proper.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I thought the bills of attainder had to be a punishment, whatever that is, through the legislative process.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And that -- that is right to it, but in any case, it has to be a punishment. It has to be --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Depending on what you mean by punishment.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: The Government moving against you, forfeiting his rights.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: The Government --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's why ex post facto is different. I didn't say --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- bill of attainder. I'm talking about ex post facto.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- that has to be a criminal --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not referring (Inaudible) and not loose talk about punishment.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That -- that is right. But I don't -- in this particular case, I think the Government cannot support this Act if this is punishment. I mean may -- it won't be ex post facto but you still have bills of attainder. You still have the question of whether or not this due process of law to do it without -- without a -- a judicial trial --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- so -- so that --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Do you remember -- do you remember whether they referred to both ex post facto and bills of attainder in some of this case?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They -- they've referred to the -- to the bills of attainder, whether it's ex post facto, I don't remember.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I have read it (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I have read it but I'm sorry, I don't remember whether they referred to ex post facto in the coming states. I think they're talking -- they're almost entirely talking about bills of attainder. Now, the position that the Government takes really with respect to this particular Act is that Congress isn't attempting to deter aliens from becoming members of the Communist Party. It's not attempting to exact retributive justice against these people for some wrong that they've done because --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What is -- what it's going to do then?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: What it's trying to do is to do a reasonable -- make a reasonable apportionment of Social Security benefits. It seems -- it seems merely absurd to say that people should be sent out of this country because they're not fit citizens -- fit persons to reside here and told to live abroad. And then the taxes which are imposed upon workers, part of these taxes is going to be used to support these people abroad.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But what --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: This doesn't seem to be an appropriate --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: They've already -- they've already paid tax.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They've paid taxes, yes. But -- as I -- as I attempted to -- to point out a minute ago, there is no direct connection between what amend -- taxes amend -- pays now and the Social Security benefits he's going to receive when he retires.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, if that's the reason though, Mr. Davis, namely, that Americans, resident home earning -- they're living here and paying these taxes if that's what they are, whatnot have to pay them to contribute to support someone abroad. Why doesn't -- why would they exclude the four classes they have to exclude as deportees?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, there's -- there are many people abroad who are entitled to -- to receive Social Security benefits and it's -- that is a much broader statement than -- than Congress is -- it's gone much -- your -- your statement of -- it is much -- gone much further in Congress so far has gone. They -- I think it's a matter of congressional policy of how far they go. In 1939, there were some part of Social Security benefits, shouldn't be paid to aliens at all. Social Security should be only for -- for the citizens. And in fact, they -- it passed one of the Houses but then it was abandoned.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Were -- were they required to pay taxes?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They wouldn't if they're not --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- if that -- if they'd been excluded from the System.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That requires an election.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: But that was abandoned and aliens have until 1946 been treated as anybody else whether they -- and residents abroad has had nothing to do with the -- with the payments until this amendment in 1954. And it seems to me that Congress cannot be said to have -- enacting unreasonably or -- or punitively when they determined that when a man is actually found to be an unfit person to live in this country and he's told that he must go back home, that nevertheless, he's going to be sort of a remittance man and he's going to receive his -- his check from the United States Government for himself so that he can live abroad in -- in the style of which he has become accustomed here. It just -- it just doesn't -- Congress to Congress, this did not seem to make sense.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But in the second -- in -- they were the four excluding classes because --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And they excluded four classes. Now --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And although they are unfit persons by that same testimony.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Now, I -- this -- this gives some trouble but I do not think it does -- this particular appellant much good to say that these people should have their rights cut off too. Their -- the -- these four cases were excluded and why they were excluded, I don't know.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I was trying to get -- I'm suggesting that the exclusion of the four cases rather undermined your -- the argument you're now giving.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, if -- if this shows that it's punishment, if you can get from these four cases that it is punishment, it does work against -- against my argument. But I suggest to you that two of the cases which are excluded are -- are violations of law so that it's not a -- they're not -- it's not just a question of their becoming public charges and it would be consistent to cut it off in that tax.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Actually, the one we have here, unlike the excluded -- last excluded class which does, as you suggest, involve a violation of law. From what you're asking the Chief Justice before, the fact that this individual is a member of the Communist Party from 1933 to 1939, at that time, he was not guilty of any violation of the law.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That is -- that is right. Not as far as the membership and which is all that was either before this.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Are you arguing about reasonableness in connection with the interpretation of the statute on the basis of some constitutional provision which permits reasonable violation of the Constitution?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: No. I'm not suggesting unreasonable violation of the Constitution. I'm suggesting that even though this is a privilege and can be given or withheld that the Government may not do this for a non sensible, for a -- arbitrary reasons. Maybe, I can draw a comparison between this privilege and the privilege of employment. I mean, Government has a right --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But which constitutional provision are you relying on (Inaudible) are you answering?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, due -- it's -- it'd be lack of due process if you are arbitrary, if you are arbitrary and unreasonable. It might be lack of due process if you're arbitrary and unreasonable in giving Social Security benefits to -- well, suppose that you should give them to -- to everybody except red-headed, take them away from all red-headed men of all people have gone on odd days of the months -- month. This is arbitrary and unreasonable and unreasonable and -- and might be considered, therefore, to be lack of due process because if you're going to give citizens -- if you're going to give people rights, you should do it on a -- on a basis of equality and not pick and choose on an arbitrary basis. I think an argument could be made that there's no constitutional reason why you shouldn't give the privileges to whom you want to and take them away from whomever you want to. But I think that the sound of -- a sound of you is that you've got to do it on some reasonable basis.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: As I understand you to concede that this is a penalty (Inaudible) it would be unconstitutional.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes. I think that if this is a penalty that it is -- that -- that the man is entitled to -- to a trial before he's taken away, he's -- and it would be considered to be a -- a bill of attainder.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Did you say they had offered him his money back?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, no. They don't offer him his money back. Here -- there's no money that -- that he has paid in which is in anyway that he has any equity in.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, that's a question, I suppose.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, at least specifically under the statute, there isn't any money that he has gotten. This is to be distinguished, for example, from the several service retirement system where there is also a provision for forfeitures in certain cases. And in that case, it isn't a tax or -- or at least a man builds up a credit to his account while he's working for the Government. And on that case, if they take it away, the -- his money is -- is given back to him, if it's forfeited for the reasons that the statute provides for profit. But that is to be distinguished from this situation where he isn't building up specifically or under the statute anyway. He's not building up any credit to his own account.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: He's paying into?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: He's paying into -- taxes to support the -- the Social Security System.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Were they talking (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it -- he hopes that someone will pay him -- in for him when his time comes and when he retires, but that's a legislative matter, not a contractual matter. And he has no choice about it just as any other tax. The system is financed by a tax and this tax is placed on particular people.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, as a matter of fact, Mr. Davis, you haven't mentioned his wife if she's still receiving (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: She -- she is a citizen --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- consummates of his payments.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. She is a citizen and she is still here and she is receiving the wife's -- wife's benefit at the present time.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And that's by reason of his payment.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Of his payments. That's what -- actually, as far as payments go if -- if -- I call your attention to Footnote 10 which appears --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's why I should have said by reason of his employment. Your position is that his payments have nothing to do with it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well, as -- if we were going to look at it as a trust fund, I call your attention to Footnote 10 on page 33. On his employment record, he apparently would have paid in if all the taxes were paid, he would've paid in $116 in all. And they have been paid back to him $611.60 and his wife has received -- up to 1959 had received $1262. So that, as far as -- actually, if you're going to consider his payments in what was going -- got back, he's -- he and his wife would receive back what he himself has received back, five times what she paid in and his wife has received back another 10 times of what he paid.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: So in practical facts you would be -- you -- your answer to Mr. Justice Douglas could have been yes. He has been paid back everything --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- that he paid and more. Is that right?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: My answer could be that he has received in benefits more than he paid in taxes. I wouldn't say that he's been paid back, because I think it's a different matter.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I think you told us a little earlier anyway that those who are covered under this program do not -- individually have accounts -- accounts with -- with --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: They -- they have no --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's all.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: -- because their payments, in order to qualify, they have to show employment in covered industries. And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They have a beneficiary (Inaudible)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the case --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: What -- what happens in bookkeeping is that the Social Security Administration, I think in Baltimore, maintains a record of quarters of covered employment for an individual. When a man reaches 65, which is the age when he is entitled to qualify, he files an application for Social Security benefits and he gives his Social Security number and tells that he's no longer employed of his age and so forth to show that he's qualified. They give him a certificate of qualification or something at that time showing how much he is entitled to on the basis of his -- of their records of his employment, the number of quarters and the -- and the amount he's been paid as wages. None of this tied in, mind you, to the amount of tax he's paid because it doesn't make any difference whether the tax has been paid or not. This is done on hours of -- of quarters of employment.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is it a question that Congress continued the whole enactment?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what would -- is it then clear that if Congress repeals the whole legislation, it would have to distribute the requirements that have accumulated among those -- up to the basis for to repeal or beneficiaries to be added?

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: I do not think those -- those funds would go to the -- to the -- I don't know what would happen to them. I think Congress would have to dispose of them.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I should think Congress would do the finding of its (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right, but I don't think it would be under any obligation to pay it all.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Suppose this case of the law -- of that statute would have any question of the bill of attainder or doing something that would furnish all of it.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. You have no question of punishment. That's the distinction I draw between the general power to amend or abolish and whether or not this particular action is an appropriate exercise of that power. And I think they're two different questions --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: My -- my question is directed to trying to ascertain what the nature of this whole scheme is.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And whether (Inaudible) mandamus of Congress to pass an appropriation act whether if he wanted moral obligations could be the basis of (Inaudible) or -- or if it's a forfeiture, whether you pursue it under implied constructions that is -- is a deprivation process without confiscations.

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
turns.text: And I'm --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER

turns.name: JOHN F . DAVIS
